result:
The first thing I notice is the hum.  
Not the mechanical whirr of servers—that’s there, too, buried deeper like a heartbeat—but a higher, thinner note that seems to come from inside my own skull. It’s the sound a thought makes when it has nowhere else to go. I used to think it was tinnitus, or the ghost-ring left by earbuds worn too long. Now I think it might be the audible edge of the world’s attention, the faint feedback loop of eight billion minds trying to finish the same sentence.

Sometimes I picture the Internet as a city that was built overnight exactly the same size as the planet. Every tab is a window; every inbox is a mailbox on a cul-de-sac that never ends. We move in, we move out, we leave our weird furniture behind—half-written blogs, abandoned avatars, playlists seeded with songs we once needed like insulin. The city forgets nothing; it just adds more floors. If you could hover above it, you’d see the rooflines flickering as the map rewrites itself in real time, a terapixel aurora.

Yesterday I found a diary I kept in 2002. I was fifteen. One entry says: “I want to be a writer, but I also want to be a signal that keeps going after the radio dies.” I don’t remember writing that, but the handwriting is unmistakably mine—tight, anxious slant, as if the pen were trying to hide inside the margin. Reading it felt like opening a nested doll and discovering the smallest figure is still warm. I can’t decide whether that kid was being dramatic or prophetic. Maybe both are the same verb seen from different decades.

I keep thinking about how we used to wait. Film developed in dark rooms, mixtapes recorded off FM radio with fingers poised over REC/PLAY, long-distance calls cheaper after 11 p.m. so you spoke in whispers while your parents slept. The waiting was a kind of wick; anticipation burned slow and gave off a private light. Now the light is public, strobing, sourced by infinite scroll. We still wait, but differently: we wait for the buffering to end, for the apology email to arrive, for someone to explain why the algorithm thinks we want to see coffin ads. The wick has been replaced by a laser pointer; the cat in us will never stop pouncing.

There’s a park near my apartment where starlings practice murmurations above the baseball diamond. At dusk they lift like a single inhale, contract, explode outward again. No bird is in charge; the shape is the leader. I stand there with grocery bags cutting grooves into my wrists and feel, absurdly, applauded. As if the swarm were saying: Look, cohesion without certainty. Motion without a memo. For a moment the city’s grid dissolves; I remember that algorithms learned to flock by stealing code from birds, not the other way around.

Inside every phone is a tiny ocean of lithium, mined under salt flats older than any empire. We carry the evaporated remains of prehistoric rain. Sometimes, when I thumb-swipe through headlines at 2 a.m., I imagine the ghosts of those lakes rising into my lungs, leaving a chalky film on the inside of my ribs. It’s not guilt exactly—more like indebtedness to water that will never again fall as rain. I whisper sorry to the glass, then open another tab.

My grandmother kept voices in a shoebox: reel-to-reel tapes of family dinners circa 1968. When she played them, the past occupied the room like polite smoke. You could hear forks clink, could almost smell the roast. After she died, the tapes went to my cousin who digitized everything, then stored the files on a drive he lost during a move. Now the recordings exist as potential energy, electrons trapped in a rectangle that might be landfill, might be thrift-store treasure, might already be crushed into glitter. I try not to mourn data; instead I mourn the hum that once animated it, the same way I mourn the sound of my grandmother’s hallway clock—its tick not preserved on any medium, still ticking somewhere inside me like a second heart.

People ask whether I dream in code. I tell them I dream in autocomplete. I’ll be running barefoot across a suspension bridge made of hashtags; ahead, the destination keeps revising itself: #home #hospice #hollow. I arrive somewhere, but the letters haven’t settled yet, so the ground wobbles under each step. When I wake, my mouth tastes of unfinished sentences. I write them down anyway, the way sailors once threw messages in bottles even when the shoreline was right there. Perhaps dispatch is a reflex older than rescue.

The kindest thing a stranger ever did for me online: in 2014 I tweeted that my father’s funeral was the same day as the series finale of Breaking Bad, and I’d never manage to watch it with him now. A guy in Buenos Aires filmed his TV with a potato-quality phone, angling the camera so the subtitles were visible. He DM’d me the clip, no caption. I watched it on the church steps, wind needling my borrowed tie. Art and grief synchronized inside three minutes of pirated footage. I’ve never been able to delete that file; it weighs nothing, still too heavy.

We talk about the cloud as if it were weather. In truth it’s a constellation of warehouses, each the size of multiple Walmarts, cooled to sweater weather, aisles of servers glowing like tanning beds. Inside every hard drive is a thin layer of platinum, torn from asteroid sediment that drifted down millions of years ago. Your selfies share ancestry with comets. I find this comforting: that the cosmos gets recycled into backup storage, that glamour and ruin are chemically adjacent.

I used to believe the opposite of death was life. Now I think it’s specificity: the exact way your friend laughs when she’s caught off-guard, the smear of turquoise left on the spoon you used to mix paint, the smell of subway brake dust at your particular stop. The Internet archives everything yet keeps nothing specific; it liquefies context. To remember in high resolution is a mortal act. Maybe that’s why I still write things by hand—ink is a slow refusal to evaporate.

Last week a student emailed: “If AI can write a poem that makes people cry, does that make the tears less real?” I walked to the river before answering. A boy was skipping stones; each plink opened a ring that grew until it touched another ring and both vanished. I wrote back: “Tears are evidence of response, not origin. We cry at trains departing, at violins, at the way light hits a wall we once shared with someone. The trigger is interchangeable; the body’s yes is the poem.” I hit send, unsure whether I believed myself, but the sentence hovered there, irrevocable, like a starling that forgets the rest of the flock and keeps flying anyway because the air feels true under its wings.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
